summary: McKenzie shares a small but meaningful moment: discovering an archived mailing list where educators were enthusiastically recommending Bingo Card Creator for teaching reading. He deliberately doesn't link to the discussion out of respect for the participants' privacy, noting that most people don't realize how much of their online activity is searchable. He reflects that while the income and intellectual challenge are nice, the most satisfying aspect is knowing his software genuinely helps teachers and kids.
key_points: Monitoring backlinks can surface genuine unsolicited endorsements from real users
Most people don't yet understand how thoroughly Google indexes and exposes their online conversations
The deepest satisfaction in building software comes from seeing it meaningfully used, not from revenue alone
money_quotes: Awareness of how much Google does to upend the expectation of privacy hasn't quite seeped in yet.
I'm most happy when folks mention how psyched they are about using it to teach their kids.
key_insight: The most rewarding signal for a small software business isn't revenue but discovering that real users genuinely love what you've built.
